dialogue.utterance: Unit 74 respond to a report of a diabetic emergency at a residence on the south side.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I just feel very weak. It came on after I went to the bathroom a few hours ago.
Dispatcher mentioned they were concerned about a diabetic issue. Do you have a history of high or low blood sugar?
Can you tell me your name, please?
Open your eyes for me, can you do that now?
What is the date today?
If I point at your hand, can you lift it for me?
We've verified your airway is patent, and you are not in a state of airway distress.
Ma'am, we're going to check your breathing first.
Pulse is 93, respirations at 22.
You mentioned weakness, anything else going on besides that?
Tell me about any allergies you may have to medications or foods.
What medications do you currently take at home?
Can you follow my finger with your eyes?
Pulse is 93, regular and palpable.
Respirations are at 22, shallow and even.
Blood pressure is 71 over 46.
Blood sugar is 432.
ECG shows sinus rhythm.
Oxygen saturation is 95%.
Your pupils are equal and reactive to light, they appear to be functioning well.
You're alert and aware of your surroundings with normal short and long-term recall.
When was the last time you had anything to eat or drink?
She's been in town to visit her daughter at the hospital. She's been feeling overwhelmed and missed her medications.
Skin looks fine, pink and warm, no wounds or rashes noted.
Breathing sounds from your lungs are clear and consistent with equal chest rise bilaterally.
Your belly feels soft and there's no pain reported.
All of your limbs exhibit normal gross motor and sensory function, and your hips and pelvis are stable.
Your airway is open and patent with no signs of obstruction detected.
Protection, movement, sensation are intact in all extremities and there are no deformities.
You're not experiencing any pain at this time.
We're going to attempt to start an IV in an extremity vein to help manage your condition.
For your condition, we've started protocol for hyperglycemic emergencies.
Pulse is 92, still within the same range.
Breathing rate hasn't changed, still 22 breaths per minute.
We're initiating continuous 4-lead ECG monitoring to assess for any cardiovascular changes.
Blood pressure is now 71 over 35.
Oxygen levels are 95%. No changes noted.
Reconfirming your ECG rhythm, still sinus.
At this time, your pulse is now 92, breathing at 22.
Your blood pressure has increased to 159 over 115.
Oxygen saturation has gone down slightly to 94%.
Currently, your breathing is down to 20 and oxygen has improved to 97%.
Blood pressure is now 148 over 87 but otherwise remains stable.
Per your husband, you missed your medications for about two days while visiting your daughter.
Your GCS is 14 - eye opening 4, verbal 4, motor 6, indicating you are fully responsive.
You have a history of breast cancer, hypertension, diabetes, and COPD - and you're insulin dependent.
VCU Medical Center is the most appropriate emergency care facility for you.
We're now going to assist you onto the stretcher and secure you for safe transport.
You're still reporting no pain and remaining stable.
Your ECG remains in sinus rhythm and oxygen saturation has returned to 97%.
There has been no change in patient condition during the entire transport to the hospital.
She is now safely seated with a five-point harness-with full support to ensure transport safety.
Final vital signs before transfer: pulse 91, respirations 20, blood pressure 148/87, oxygen 97%.
Continuous EKG readings remain stable, with no rhythm changes or deviations detected.
She is stable and prepared for potential onward care at the ED.
Transfer to hospital without incident-ready for pickup by VCU staff.
All critical information has been documented and we're now turning you over to the hospital team.
Final assessment under current conditions remains consistent with hyperglycemia and no immediate pain reported.
